name: Safety Stock (lead-time variability method)
description: Buffer stock when both demand and lead time vary: a service-level factor times the combined standard deviation of demand over the lead time, which pools demand variance across the lead time with lead-time variance scaled by the demand rate.

inputs.name: Service-level factor
inputs.description: Safety factor for the target service level (see reference.service_level_z_factors).

outputs.name: Combined standard deviation of lead-time demand
outputs.description: Standard deviation of demand during the lead time when both demand and lead time vary.

expression: sigma_L = sqrt(LT * sigma_d^2 + D^2 * sigma_LT^2); SS = z * sigma_L
assumptions: Demand variability and lead-time variability are independent of each other.
Lead-time demand is approximately normally distributed.
Demand and lead time use the same time unit (e.g. per-day demand with lead time in days).
limitations: If lead time is constant (sigma_LT = 0), this reduces to the basic service-level form SS = z * sigma_d * sqrt(LT).
The service-level factor assumes a cycle-service-level target, not a fill-rate target.
Correlated demand and lead time (e.g. shortages that lengthen lead times during demand spikes) understate the required buffer.

examples.note: Distribution Planning Exercise 8.5 (ABC Electronics): 95% service level (z = 1.65), D = 250 units/day, sigma_d = 85, LT = 9 days, sigma_LT = 5 days. sigma_L = sqrt(9 x 85^2 + 250^2 x 5^2) = 1,275.74; SS = 1.65 x 1,275.74 = 2,105 (rounded).

citation.quote: sigma_L = sqrt([9 x (85^2)] + [(250^2) x (5^2)]) = 1,275.74 ... To achieve a CSL of 95%, 1.65 is chosen. To arrive at the new safety stock, the standard deviation of demand during the lead time is then multiplied by the safety factor, or 1.65 x 1,275 = 2,105 (rounded).

sources: Chopra-Meindl combined demand/lead-time variability safety-stock model

citations.quote: The first step is to calculate the standard deviation of demand during the lead time (sigma_L) using the formula: sigma_L = sqrt(LT x sigma_D^2 + D^2 x sigma_LT^2)
The required supply safety stocks for an service level of 99% are then calculated as Safety factor x sqrt((standard deviation_quantity)^2 + (standard deviation_lead time x demand rate)^2)
